title: Why Your Compost Is Not Heating Up
excerpt: A compost pile that stays cool is not always failing, but it often means one or more important conditions are missing. Pile size, moisture, airflow, and the balance of greens and browns all affect whether compost heats up or breaks down more slowly.
author: Permatable Team
content:
Compost Heat

A compost pile that heats up is often a sign that decomposition is happening quickly and actively. So when a pile stays cool, many gardeners assume something is wrong. In some cases, that is true. But in other cases, a cool pile simply means the compost is breaking down slowly rather than rapidly.

If your compost is not heating up, the usual cause is that one or more key conditions are missing. Heat in a compost pile depends on the right mix of materials, enough moisture, enough air, and enough pile size to support active microbial life.

The good news is that most heating problems can be fixed.

Why Compost Heats Up in the First Place

Compost gets warm because microorganisms are breaking down organic matter. As they feed and multiply, they generate heat.

A hot compost pile usually has:

  • Enough nitrogen-rich green material
  • Enough carbon-rich brown material
  • The right amount of moisture
  • Plenty of oxygen
  • Enough volume to hold heat

If one of these factors is off, the pile may stay cool or only warm slightly.

Is It Bad if Compost Does Not Heat Up?

Not always. Compost does not have to get hot to work.

There are two main styles of composting:

Hot Composting

This method is faster and depends on the pile heating up significantly. It requires more active management.

Cold Composting

This method is slower and often stays cool. Materials break down gradually over time with less effort.

So if your compost is cool but still shrinking and slowly decomposing, it may simply be cold composting. That is still valid composting. But if you want faster results, heating becomes more important.

1. Your Pile Is Too Small

One of the most common reasons compost does not heat up is that the pile is too small to hold heat.

A very small bin or pile loses warmth quickly, especially in cool weather. Microbial activity may be happening, but not at a level that creates noticeable heat.

How to Fix It

Try building a pile that is at least around 3 feet wide, 3 feet long, and 3 feet high if space allows. A larger mass holds moisture and heat more effectively.

2. There Are Not Enough Green Materials

Greens provide nitrogen, which fuels the microorganisms responsible for fast decomposition. If the pile is made mostly of dry leaves, cardboard, or other browns, it may not have enough energy to heat up.

Common green materials include:

  • Fruit and vegetable scraps
  • Coffee grounds
  • Grass clippings
  • Fresh plant trimmings
  • Tea leaves

How to Fix It

Add more greens and mix them into the pile. Be careful not to add too much at once without balancing with browns.

3. There Are Too Many Browns

Browns are important, but a pile that is mostly carbon-rich material may decompose very slowly.

Too many browns can leave the compost:

  • Dry
  • Cool
  • Slow to break down
  • Lacking microbial activity

How to Fix It

Add more nitrogen-rich materials and turn the pile to blend everything evenly.

4. The Pile Is Too Dry

Microorganisms need moisture to survive and work. If the compost is dry, decomposition slows down, and heat production drops.

A dry pile may look dusty, feel light, and show little visible change over time.

How to Fix It

Add water gradually while turning the pile. The compost should feel like a wrung-out sponge, moist but not soaked.

5. The Pile Is Too Wet

Too much moisture can also prevent heating. A soggy compost pile loses airflow, and without oxygen, beneficial microbes struggle.

Signs of too much moisture include:

  • A heavy, compacted feel
  • Slimy materials
  • Unpleasant odors
  • Little internal warmth

How to Fix It

Add dry browns such as leaves, shredded cardboard, or paper. Then turn the pile to loosen it and improve airflow.

6. There Is Not Enough Air

Hot composting depends on oxygen. If the pile is compacted or never turned, microbes do not get enough air to stay highly active.

This is especially common when:

  • Grass clippings are added in thick layers
  • Wet food scraps form clumps
  • The pile is packed too tightly

How to Fix It

Turn the pile with a fork or shovel to break up dense sections. Mixing in coarse browns can also help create air pockets.

7. The Materials Are Too Large

Large branches, whole food scraps, and thick stems break down slowly. If most of the pile consists of bulky material, microbial activity may not be strong enough to create much heat.

How to Fix It

Chop or shred materials before adding them. Smaller pieces decompose faster and create more surface area for microbial activity.

8. The Weather Is Too Cold

In cooler months, compost naturally slows down. Even a well-built pile may struggle to heat up in winter or during cold, wet weather.

This does not always mean the pile is failing. It may simply be responding to seasonal conditions.

How to Fix It

Insulate the pile with straw or extra leaves, keep it large enough to hold heat, and be patient. Composting often speeds up again in warmer weather.

9. You Added Materials Too Slowly

A hot compost pile usually heats best when built all at once or in large enough batches. If you add a small amount here and there, the pile may never build enough active mass to heat significantly.

How to Fix It

Try collecting materials and building a larger batch at one time, or continue composting more slowly with the understanding that it may stay cool.

10. The Pile Has Finished Its Hottest Stage

Sometimes a pile does heat up, but only briefly. If you check it later, it may already have cooled down.

This is normal. Compost often goes through cycles of heating and cooling, especially if it is turned or if the most active materials have already broken down.

How to Fix It

Turn the pile and see whether it warms again. If it does, the compost is still active. If not, it may simply be moving into a slower stage.

How to Tell if a Cool Pile Is Still Working

Even if your compost is not hot, it may still be decomposing.

Signs of progress include:

  • The pile slowly shrinks
  • Materials darken over time
  • Scraps become less recognizable
  • The smell is earthy, not rotten
  • Worms or other decomposers are present

These are all good signs, even without strong heat.

How to Encourage a Hotter Compost Pile

If your goal is faster compost, focus on these basics:

  • Build a larger pile
  • Add a good mix of greens and browns
  • Keep the pile moist
  • Turn it regularly
  • Chop materials smaller
  • Avoid letting it get compacted

These adjustments often make a noticeable difference.

Final Thoughts

If your compost is not heating up, the cause is usually something simple like pile size, moisture, airflow, or an imbalance of greens and browns. In many cases, a few small changes are enough to restart microbial activity and raise the temperature.

And remember, not all compost has to be hot to succeed. A cool pile can still produce good compost. It just does the job more slowly.
